role_summary: Co-founder (with husband Rev. Charles Sherrod, catalog entry 18) of New Communities Inc, the 1969 Lee County, Georgia collective farm modeled on Israeli kibbutzim that pioneered the U.S. community-land-trust movement; USDA Georgia State Director of Rural Development under President Obama (2009-2010); subject of the July 2010 USDA-Breitbart selective-editing incident.
ai_reading: Sherrod's top semantic neighbor is Sam Young Jr. (entry 114) at a cosine count of 156; the broader cluster includes Linda Fuller Degelmann (entry 78, Habitat for Humanity co-founder), James Oscar Jones (entry 57), and H. Jack Geiger (entry 54, Mississippi community-medicine). The cluster is the rural-cooperative-and-economic-justice cohort, the catalog's distinct rural-economic-development-cohort signature. The catalog flags a +0.74 reading on the Tactical Pragmatism vs Strategic Vision axis (far toward Strategic Vision) as the most-distinctive structural reading. For a forty-year career in Black-owned cooperative agriculture, the embedding's pull toward Strategic Vision is consistent with the long-arc institutional work the New Communities cooperative represents; the Strategic Vision signature here is the catalog's clearest example of an institutional-multidecade-organizing vocabulary distinguishable from the single-event direct-action register most of the catalog's other figures inhabit.
biographical_paragraph: While Sherrod's LoC oral history carries a publication-block audit tier (known issues prevent full publication-grade certification, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external sources before citation), her external biographical record is well-documented [src: 1]. Sherrod was born July 7, 1947 in Newton, Georgia, the eldest of five children of Hosie and Grace Miller; her father was murdered by a white neighbor in 1965 over a property dispute that an all-white grand jury declined to prosecute, a foundational event in her commitment to civil rights organizing [src: 2]. She organized voter-registration work in southwest Georgia from her teens; she married Rev. Charles Sherrod (catalog entry 18, the founding SNCC Albany Movement organizer) in the late 1960s. Together with her husband she co-founded New Communities Inc in 1969 in Lee County, Georgia, the kibbutzim-modeled Black-owned collective farm that across its 1969-1985 active years pioneered the U.S. community-land-trust movement [src: 2]. After New Communities's 1985 USDA-discrimination-driven foreclosure she continued cooperative-agriculture organizing and in 2009 was appointed USDA Georgia State Director of Rural Development by President Barack Obama. In July 2010 she was wrongfully fired from that position after Andrew Breitbart published a selectively edited video of her NAACP Atlanta speech that made her appear to admit racial discrimination; the full speech showed her telling the opposite story (a moment of past prejudice she had worked through to expand her own racial-equity practice). The Obama administration apologized publicly within days; she declined the reoffered USDA position but accepted a White House civil-rights consultancy. The New Communities cooperative was awarded $13 million in 1999 in the Pigford v. Glickman USDA-discrimination settlement, the resources for which they have since used to purchase a 1,638-acre farm in Albany, Georgia [src: 2].

sources.title: Shirley Miller Sherrod oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)

sources.title: New Communities Inc institutional history (the cooperative the Sherrods co-founded in 1969)
